mission.title: Install and Configure Kubevela on Kubernetes
mission.description: KubeVela is a modern application delivery platform that simplifies deploying and managing applications across hybrid and multi-cloud environments. By using KubeVela, you can enhance your application deployment workflows and improve operational efficiency.

steps.title: Add the KubeVela Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official KubeVela Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add kubevela https://charts.kubevela.net/core
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install KubeVela using Helm
steps.description:
Install KubeVela in a new namespace called 'kubevela' with the specified version:
```bash
helm install vela-core kubevela/vela-core --version v1.10.7 --namespace kubevela --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the KubeVela pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kubevela
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Update the KubeVela deployment to set resource limits for the controller:
```bash
kubectl patch deployment vela-core --namespace kubevela --type='json' -p='[{"op": "add", "path": "/spec/template/spec/containers/0/resources", "value": {"limits": {"cpu": "500m", "memory": "512Mi"}, "requests": {"cpu": "250m", "memory": "256Mi"}}}]'
```

steps.title: Set up RBAC for KubeVela
steps.description:
Create a Role and RoleBinding to allow access to KubeVela resources:
```bash
kubectl create role kubevela-admin --verb=* --resource=* --namespace kubevela
kubectl create rolebinding kubevela-admin-binding --role=kubevela-admin --user=system:serviceaccount:kubevela:default --namespace kubevela
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of KubeVela will show the KubeVela pods in the 'kubevela' namespace as running. You will also have configured resource limits and RBAC for managing access to KubeVela resources.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kubevela https://charts.kubevela.net/core
helm repo update
helm install vela-core kubevela/vela-core --version v1.10.7 --namespace kubevela --create-namespace

kubectl create role kubevela-admin --verb=* --resource=* --namespace kubevela
kubectl create rolebinding kubevela-admin-binding --role=kubevela-admin --user=system:serviceaccount:kubevela:default --namespace kubevela

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the KubeVela release from the cluster using Helm. This will remove the deployment and associated resources created by the installation.
```bash
helm uninstall vela-core --namespace kubevela
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created during the installation. This ensures that all resources related to KubeVela are cleaned up.
```bash
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep 'kubevela.io' | awk '{print $1}')
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace kubevela
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'kubevela' namespace to remove all remaining resources and verify that it has been deleted successfully.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kubevela
kubectl get namespaces | grep kubevela
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's important to back up the current state of KubeVela. You can do this by exporting the current configuration and resources.
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace kubevela -o yaml > kubevela-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and run the upgrade command to upgrade KubeVela to the latest version. Make sure to specify the new version you want to upgrade to.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade vela-core kubevela/vela-core --version v1.10.8 --namespace kubevela
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the KubeVela pods are running the new version successfully. Check the status of the pods to ensure everything is functioning correctly.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kubevela
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the KubeVela pods are stuck in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for the failing pod to diagnose the issue.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace kubevela
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with 'release not found'
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an error stating 'release not found' during an upgrade, ensure that the release name is correct and that it exists in the specified namespace.
```bash
helm list --namespace kubevela
```

troubleshooting.title: RBAC issues preventing access
troubleshooting.description:
If you face permission issues, verify that the Role and RoleBinding are correctly set up. Check the bindings for the service account used by KubeVela.
```bash
kubectl get rolebinding --namespace kubevela
kubectl describe rolebinding kubevela-admin-binding --namespace kubevela
```

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing pod evictions
troubleshooting.description:
If pods are being evicted due to resource limits, review the resource requests and limits set for the KubeVela deployment and adjust them as necessary.
```bash
kubectl get deployment vela-core --namespace kubevela -o yaml | grep resources -A 5
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
